key_insight: A recurrent state-transition + reinforcement learning framework can adapt bladder cancer treatment recommendations over successive disease episodes instead of relying on one-time static rules.
clinical_relevance: This approach may support more personalized, trajectory-aware treatment planning for recurrent bladder cancer in precision oncology settings.
method: Predicts how tumor characteristics change after each treatment using a state-transition model.
Frames treatment selection as a Markov Decision Process and trains a Deep Q-Network agent to choose sequential actions.
Produces interpretable trajectories and simulation logs to make the decision process more transparent.
evidence_signal: In simulation, the framework reported a cumulative reward of 63,918.87, an average training loss of 0.0056 per episode, and a 6.62% policy improvement over comparison approaches.
watch_item: Results are based on simulated trajectories, so external validation in real patient datasets and prospective clinical testing are still needed.
paper_title: Enhancing Personalized Bladder Cancer Treatment Through Reinforcement Learning: A Recurrent Patient State Transition Decision Support Framework
